name: graph
context: Understand the Dojo knowledge graph — traversal, wiki-links, backlinks, related edges, and link resolution
info: Dojo is a knowledge graph, not a flat registry. Every node connects to other nodes through four link types: parent-child hierarchy, wiki-links in body text via [[uri]] syntax, directed links[] with context annotations, and semantic related[] edges with typed relations (prerequisite, see-also, implements, extends, similar, evolution, equivalent, alternative). The graph enables agents to navigate from a known node to related knowledge without searching — following links is often faster and more contextual than querying. Backlinks are computed at runtime: if node A links to node B, then B automatically knows about A. Graph traversal powers the /v1/graph endpoint, learn workflows, and agent navigation.
triggers: how does the dojo graph work
what are wiki links in dojo
body:
# Knowledge Graph

Dojo is a directed graph where nodes are connected by multiple link types. Understanding the graph enables agents to navigate between related knowledge without searching — following edges is faster and preserves context.

## Four Link Types

### 1. Parent-Child Hierarchy

Every node has a `parent` field (except ecosystem roots). The tree hierarchy is the backbone of the graph:

```
github (ecosystem)
├── repos (skill)
│   ├── create (sub)
│   └── settings (sub)
├── issues (skill)
└── auth (context)
```

Traverse up via `parent`, down via `sub[]` or directory listing.

### 2. Wiki-Links in Body Text

Any `body` or `sections[].body` field can contain `[[uri]]` wiki-links:

```markdown
See [[github/auth]] for authentication setup.
Use [[github/repos/create]] to make a new repository.
Check [[github/rate-limits#conditional-requests]] for caching.
```

Wiki-links support section targeting: `[[uri#section-id]]`.

### 3. Directed Links

The `links[]` array contains explicit next-step connections:

```json
{
  "links": [
    { "uri": "github/auth", "context": "Authentication for API access" },
    { "uri": "github/repos/create", "context": "Create a new repository" }
  ]
}
```

Links are directional: they represent "from here, you might want to go there."

### 4. Semantic Related Edges

The `related[]` array contains typed semantic relationships:

```json
{
  "related": [
    { "uri": "github/auth", "relation": "prerequisite", "note": "Auth is required" },
    { "uri": "github/pulls", "relation": "see-also", "note": "PRs reference issues" }
  ]
}
```

Relation types: `prerequisite`, `see-also`, `implements`, `extends`, `similar`, `evolution`, `equivalent`, `alternative`.

## Backlinks

Backlinks are the reverse of forward links. If node A links to node B (in any link type), then B has a backlink from A. Backlinks are computed at runtime by scanning all forward links across the graph.

```bash
# Get backlinks for a node
curl "$DOJO_URL/v1/graph/github/auth/backlinks"
# Returns all nodes that link TO github/auth
```

Backlinks answer the question: "Who references this node?" This is essential for understanding a node's impact and for pruning unused nodes.

## Graph API

```bash
# Get the full graph for a subtree
curl "$DOJO_URL/v1/graph/github"

# Get direct neighbors (links + related + parent + children)
curl "$DOJO_URL/v1/graph/github/repos/neighbors"

# Get backlinks
curl "$DOJO_URL/v1/graph/github/auth/backlinks"

# Shortest path between two nodes
curl "$DOJO_URL/v1/graph/path?from=github/repos&to=github/actions"
```

## Agent Navigation Patterns

### Depth-First Learning
Start at a known node, follow `links[]` to learn prerequisites, then return:
1. Load `kubernetes/deployments`
2. See `related: prerequisite → kubernetes/pods`
3. Load `kubernetes/pods` first
4. Return to `kubernetes/deployments` with context

### Breadth-First Exploration
From an ecosystem root, scan all children to understand scope:
1. Load `github` → read `context` for each child in `links[]`
2. Identify the most relevant skill
3. Dive into that skill

### Backlink Discovery
Find what depends on a node before changing it:
1. Query backlinks for `github/auth`
2. See that `repos`, `issues`, `pulls`, `actions` all depend on it
3. Understand the blast radius of auth changes

sections.title: Four link types
sections.body: Dojo nodes connect through four link types: (1) parent-child hierarchy via `parent` and `sub[]` fields, (2) wiki-links in body text via `[[uri]]` syntax with optional section targeting, (3) directed links in `links[]` with context annotations, and (4) semantic related edges in `related[]` with typed relations. Each link type serves a different purpose: hierarchy for structure, wiki-links for inline references, directed links for next-step navigation, and related edges for semantic reasoning.

sections.title: Backlinks
sections.body: Backlinks are the reverse of forward links — if A links to B, then B has a backlink from A. Computed at runtime by scanning all forward links. Query via `GET /v1/graph/<uri>/backlinks`. Backlinks answer 'who references this node?' and are essential for impact analysis, dependency tracking, and pruning unused nodes. Every context node should have at least one backlink from an actionable node.

sections.title: Graph API endpoints
sections.body: The graph API exposes: `GET /v1/graph/<uri>` for the full subtree graph, `GET /v1/graph/<uri>/neighbors` for direct connections (links + related + parent + children), `GET /v1/graph/<uri>/backlinks` for reverse links, and `GET /v1/graph/path?from=<uri>&to=<uri>` for shortest path between two nodes. See [[dojo/api#knowledge-routes]] for the full HTTP surface.

sections.title: Agent navigation patterns
sections.body: Three primary patterns: (1) Depth-first learning — follow prerequisite edges before the target node, (2) Breadth-first exploration — scan children of an ecosystem root to understand scope before diving in, (3) Backlink discovery — check what depends on a node before modifying it. Choose based on the agent's goal: learning uses depth-first, exploration uses breadth-first, impact analysis uses backlinks.

sections.title: Wiki-link syntax
sections.body: Wiki-links use `[[uri]]` syntax in any markdown field (body, sections[].body, info). They support section targeting: `[[github/rest#pagination]]` links to the pagination section of the github/rest node. At render time, wiki-links are resolved to full node manifests. Broken wiki-links (pointing to non-existent URIs or sections) are detected by [[dojo/validate/knowledge]].
